channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: RFK Jr. Appears Disengaged on Many Health Department Matters Beyond Vaccines
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has demonstrated little interest in managing his sprawling department as he focuses on food and vaccine policies, according to colleagues.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Kennedy, who is isolated from much of his top staff, leans on a small number of key advisers.

item.title: An Uncertain Win for Immigrants Seeking to Stay in U.S.
item.description: After a judge’s ruling, there was a sense of renewed hope that immigration applications that were put on hold would move forward. But how soon that would happen was unknown.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Immigrants waited in a hallway for their hearings at the Annandale Immigration Court last month.

item.title: Hegseth Criticizes Europe Over Migration ‘Invasion’ in D-Day Speech
item.description: The remarks from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth reflect many of the Trump administration’s previous assertions on immigrants in Europe, which overlap with the language of European far-right political parties.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Jeremias Gonzalez/Associated Press
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at the U.S. cemetery in Colleville-sur-Mer, France, at a D-Day commemoration, on Saturday.

item.title: Kennedy Center Loses Case Against Musician Who Canceled Over Trump Naming Dispute
item.description: A judge sided with jazz performer Chuck Redd, who canceled a 2025 holiday concert after President Trump’s name was added to the building.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A judge threw out a lawsuit filed by the John F. Kennedy Center against a jazz musician who canceled a Christmas Eve performance.

item.title: Trump Says Iran Has Made a ‘Big’ Nuclear Promise. It Isn’t New.
item.description: President Trump’s boasts of securing a commitment from Iranian leaders not to develop a nuclear weapon have puzzled nuclear experts who note that Tehran has made that pledge for more than 50 years.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has said the main reason he went to war on Feb. 28 was to prevent Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon.

item.title: Trump’s Defense Department Sees Growing Espionage Threat From Israel
item.description: The Defense Department has increased the counterintelligence threat assessment to its highest level, and Israel is believed to have eavesdropped on American negotiations with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu last December. The United States and Israel were largely aligned at the war’s outset, with Mr. Trump endorsing Mr. Netanyahu’s goal to oust the Iranian government.

item.title: Arab Gunman Kills One in Attack on Central Israel
item.description: The assailant, an Arab citizen of Israel, was killed. Hamas, the Palestinian militant group, praised the shooting, but it did not claim responsibility.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:credit: Jack Guez/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Israeli security forces in Kochav Yair, Israel, on Sunday, after a shooting.

item.title: Scott Pelley on the Bari Weiss Era and His Last Days at ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: Lulu Garcia-Navarro sits down for an exclusive interview with the now-former CBS News correspondent.
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Trump Looms Over Pivotal Republican Senate Runoff in Georgia
item.description: The president has not yet endorsed Representative Mike Collins or Derek Dooley, a former football coach, in the race to challenge the Democratic senator, Jon Ossoff.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times; David Walter Banks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Collins, a self-described MAGA loyalist, and Derek Dooley, a close friend of Gov. Brian Kemp, will face each other in a Republican runoff for Senate in Georgia on June 16.

item.title: South Carolina Governor’s Race Tests the Strength of a Trump Endorsement
item.description: The Republican candidates for South Carolina governor went to extraordinary lengths for the president’s support. But his choice of Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette has not quite sealed the deal among voters.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina and Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The candidates for governor in South Carolina went to extraordinary lengths to garner President Trump’s endorsement.

item.title: The Bidens Return With New Book and South Dakota Speech Targeting Trump
item.description: The re-emergence of the former first family has been fraught for some Democrats who just want to move on.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Matt Kelley/Associated Press
item.media:description: Former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. at a South Carolina Democratic Party rally in February.

item.title: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle CEO, Confessed to Fraud. The Board Let Her Stay in Charge.
item.description: The final months of the fashion tech start-up CaaStle included fake audits, stock buybacks and a damage control strategy led by the chief executive who defrauded it.
item.dc:creator: Anita Raghavan
item.media:credit: Larry Neumeister/Associated Press
item.media:description: Christine Hunsicker, CaaStle’s co-founder, pleaded guilty to securities fraud in March.

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards: What to Expect
item.description: This year’s annual celebration of the best on Broadway is being hosted by Pink.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The CBS telecast of the Tonys introduces the latest plays and musicals to a television audience of several million.

item.title: June Squibb, the ‘Foulest Mouth on Broadway’, Is Up for a Tony at 96
item.description: At 96, June Squibb is one of the oldest acting nominees in the history of the Tony Awards. Back in the 1960s, she was known for an entirely different distinction.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: June Squibb has received her first Tony nomination, for her featured role in “Marjorie Prime.”

item.title: How to Watch the 2026 Tony Awards
item.description: The ceremony, at Radio City Music Hall, will be broadcast on CBS starting at 8 p.m. Eastern, and livestreamed on Paramount+ for premium subscribers.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Daniel Knighton/Getty Images
item.media:description: Pink will host he 79th Tony Awards at Radio City Hall. The ceremony, which will be broadcast live on CBS and is available to stream on Paramount+, begins at 8 p.m. Eastern.

item.title: Americans Love Soccer Stars Who Aren’t Made in America
item.description: The United States has gone wild over foreign-born players on several occasions. Now the largest World Cup ever is arriving.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Megan Briggs/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Argentine star Lionel Messi is about to play in his sixth World Cup. His pink Inter Miami jersey became a hot item when he began playing in the United States in 2023.

item.title: Ahead of World Cup, Two Generations of Haitian Players Unite
item.description: A player from Haiti’s 1974 World Cup team, the last to qualify for the tournament, met with the co-captain of this year’s squad in South Florida.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ricardo Adé, Haitian professional soccer player for the country’s national team, right, greets former Haitian national team player Ernst Jean-Baptiste at a restaurant in Miami, Fla., on Saturday.

item.title: What Steven Spielberg Taught Me About Fear, Catharsis, and Being Human
item.description: Hollywood is struggling, but Spielberg insists that the big screen is still the best place to work out our collective dreams, joys and sorrows.
item.dc:creator: Wesley Morris
item.media:credit: Paolo Pellegrin/Magnum, for The New York Times
item.media:description: “For years, I was working out my mom and dad’s divorce through my stories,” Spielberg says.

item.title: Who Will Account for Another Foreign Policy Failure?
item.description: The American foreign policy class should shed its addiction to military force.
item.dc:creator: Peter Beinart
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
American Enterprise Institute for Public Policy Research

item.title: The Betrayal of Black Voters Threatens Our Democracy
item.description: The sweeping effort to dismantle Black-majority congressional districts in the South will have far-reaching consequences for all Americans, and for our democracy.
item.dc:creator: Mara Gay
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Touching Grass
item.description: There’s no better time than early summer to put away your phone and get some fresh air. We have a challenge to help you actually do it.
item.dc:creator: Jancee Dunn

item.title: A New Governor Faces Her First Crisis Amid Tear Gas and Tough Decisions
item.description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill dispatched the New Jersey State Police outside the Delaney Hall migrant detention center after federal officials said they would flood the area with armed agents. Not everyone approved.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill of New Jersey and Senator Andy Kim tried to visit the Delaney Hall immigration detention center on Memorial Day. Senator Kim was allowed to enter. The governor wasn’t.
